title: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: A comprehensive two-stage discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) compiled by Gemini 3.1. Economic Prospect Score: 73/100 — Strong Prospect.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: For financial institutions, we discount equity cash flows by the Cost of Equity, calculated via the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). Using a 10-Year Treasury risk-free rate of 4.18%, a beta of 1.12, and an equity risk premium of 4.8%.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: In the terminal phase (Year 6 onwards), we assume the bank grows perpetually at 2.5%, roughly matching long-term target inflation and nominal GDP growth in the United States.

verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 18, 2026).

items.title: Net Interest Margin Compression
items.description: A rapidly declining rate environment could squeeze net interest income, which has heavily bolstered JPM's profits in recent years.

items.title: Regulatory Capital Rules (Basel III Endgame)
items.description: Stricter capital requirements force banks to hold more capital against assets. This directly reduces the percentage of Net Income that can be classified as FCFE and returned to shareholders.

items.title: Macroeconomic Deterioration
items.description: An unexpected recession would lead to a spike in provision for credit losses (PCL) and consumer defaults, severely impacting the base year Net Income used for projections.

faq.question: Why is JPMorgan's standard Free Cash Flow negative?
faq.answer: Banks take in deposits and lend them out. In cash flow statements, an increase in loans (an asset) is counted as a cash outflow. If a bank is growing its loan book aggressively, its operating cash flow often turns deeply negative. This is a sign of business growth, not financial distress, which is why standard FCF is inappropriate for bank valuation.

faq.question: What is Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)?
faq.answer: FCFE measures the cash left over after taxes, capital expenditures, and debt repayments, minus the capital needed to maintain required regulatory ratios. It represents the maximum theoretical amount a bank could pay out in dividends and share buybacks without harming its operational stability.

faq.question: Why use Cost of Equity instead of WACC?
faq.answer: For non-financial companies, we discount Unlevered FCF using the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). However, for a bank, debt is not just capital; it is raw material (deposits). Because we are projecting cash flows specifically available to equity holders (FCFE), we must discount them using only the Cost of Equity.

prospectVerdictDetail: JPMorgan is the best-run bank in the world — fortress balance sheet, #1 investment bank, dominant consumer franchise. The $15B+/yr tech investment creates competitive separation. The risk is cyclical: credit losses in recession and NII normalizing. But if you own one bank, this is the one.
prospectKeyRisks: Credit cycle deterioration — consumer delinquencies rising, recession would increase loan loss provisions
Net interest income declining as Fed potentially cuts rates
Jamie Dimon's eventual retirement creating succession uncertainty
prospectKeyCatalysts: Market share gains from regional banks struggling with CRE exposure
JPMorgan Payments and embedded finance growing to $10B+ revenue
Investment banking fee recovery as M&A and IPO activity normalizes
prospectMethodology: Score is based on three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30), totaling 0-100.
